children.text: Asides (also called callouts or admonitions) display secondary information alongside the main content. They come in four variants with distinct colors and icons.

content.html:
FieldTypeDescription
`type`string`note`, `tip`, `caution`, or `danger`
`title`stringCustom title (defaults to the type name if empty)
`content`stringThe callout body text

children.text: (yellow) — important caveats the reader should be aware of
(red) — warnings about destructive or irreversible actions
In markdown mode, asides use the

content.code:
:::note
This is a note.
:::

:::tip[Custom title]
Tips can have custom titles.
:::

:::caution
Be careful with this.
:::

:::danger
This action cannot be undone.
:::

children.text: and an optional title can be placed in square brackets.

content.content: This is a note aside. It displays general supplementary information.

content.title: Pro tip
content.content: You can use asides to highlight important information that readers might otherwise miss.
Asides in markdown mode must have matching opening and closing `:::` fences.
Deleting a page from the admin cannot be undone.

children.text: Badges are small inline labels for showing status, version numbers, or categories.

content.html:
FieldTypeDescription
`text`stringThe badge label text
`variant`string`default`, `note`, `tip`, `caution`, `danger`, or `success`
`size`string`small`, `medium`, or `large`

children.text: Add a badge block from the admin block picker or edit an existing one via the inline block editor. Badges render as pill-shaped labels inline with surrounding content.
Badges are most useful when added alongside headings or within card content to indicate status:
Card grids arrange multiple cards in a responsive column layout.

content.html:
FieldTypeDescription
`id`stringOptional section ID for linking
`cardsJson`stringJSON array of card objects

children.text: array has the same fields as a standalone
Card grids are commonly used on landing pages or feature overview sections. The grid automatically adjusts the number of columns based on the viewport width.
To edit a card grid, hover over it in Edit mode and click
Cards group related content in a visually distinct box with an optional icon and color accent.

content.html:
FieldTypeDescription
`title`stringCard heading
`icon`stringIcon name from the [icon set](/reference/icons)
`color`stringAccent color: `purple`, `orange`, `green`, `red`, `blue`
`content`stringCard body text

children.text: Add a card from the admin block picker. The title and icon appear at the top of the card, with the content below. Cards are useful for highlighting features, summarizing concepts, or creating visual groupings.
Cards can be placed individually or inside a
A card with an icon looks like this:
With Edit mode on, hover over a card and click
to modify its title, icon, color, and content inline.
Code blocks render syntax-highlighted code using
with bundled Night Owl dark and light themes.

content.html:
FieldTypeDescription
`language`stringLanguage identifier (e.g. `ts`, `bash`, `json`, `css`)
`code`stringThe code content

children.text: In markdown mode, code blocks use standard fenced code block syntax:
The language identifier after the opening fence determines syntax highlighting.
Expressive Code supports all languages that Shiki supports, including:
Star-Lite bundles Night Owl dark and light themes by default. You can customize this via the
to disable the bundled integration entirely.
File trees render directory structures with file-type icons, indentation, and visual hierarchy.

content.html:
FieldTypeDescription
`treeHtml`stringTree content as indented list text

children.text: The tree content uses a dash-indented list format:

content.code:
- src/
  - pages/
    - index.astro
    - about.astro
  - components/
    - Header.astro
- package.json
- astro.config.mjs

children.text: are rendered as directories (with folder icons). Other entries are rendered as files with icons based on their extension.
Add a file tree from the admin block picker and enter the tree content in the
field. The indentation determines nesting depth.
File trees are useful for explaining project structure, showing expected directory layouts, or illustrating where files should be placed.
The icon block renders an inline SVG icon from Star-Lite's built-in icon set (inherited from Starlight).

content.html:
FieldTypeDescription
`name`stringIcon name (see [Icons Reference](/reference/icons))
`label`stringAccessible label for screen readers
`color`stringIcon color
`size`stringIcon size

children.text: Add an icon block from the admin block picker. Icons render inline and can be placed alongside text content.
Icons are also used as properties in other block types:
Star-Lite includes 93 icons covering UI elements, actions, and brand logos. See the
Link buttons render links as visually prominent pill-shaped buttons, commonly used for calls to action.

content.html:
FieldTypeDescription
`text`stringButton label
`href`stringLink URL
`icon`stringOptional icon name from the [icon set](/reference/icons)
`iconPlacement`string`start` or `end` (default: `end`)
`variant`string`primary`, `secondary`, or `minimal`

children.text: — filled background with accent color, white text
— text-only with no border or background
Link buttons are useful for navigation prompts, download links, or any action you want to draw attention to. They're commonly placed after introductory text or in hero sections.

content.code:
{
  "_type": "star-lite.linkButton",
  "text": "Get Started",
  "href": "/getting-started",
  "icon": "right-arrow",
  "variant": "primary"
}

children.text: Link cards render a link as a styled card with a title, description, and arrow indicator — useful for directing readers to related pages.

content.html:
FieldTypeDescription
`id`stringOptional section ID
`title`stringCard title
`href`stringLink URL
`description`stringDescription text below the title
`target`stringLink target (e.g. `_blank` for new tab)

children.text: Link cards are ideal for "next steps" sections, related pages, or navigation between sections of your docs. They render as full-width clickable cards with the title, description, and a directional arrow.
Steps render a numbered list with visual step indicators, ideal for tutorials and how-to guides.

content.html:
FieldTypeDescription
`start`numberStarting step number (default: 1)
`stepsJson`stringJSON array of `{ content }` objects

children.text: Add a steps block from the admin block picker and enter the steps JSON. Each step is rendered with a numbered circle indicator and the content text.
Steps are best used for sequential instructions where order matters — installation guides, setup procedures, or multi-part tutorials.
Tabs group equivalent content into switchable panels — useful when showing the same task across different tools, languages, or platforms.

content.html:
FieldTypeDescription
`id`stringOptional section ID
`tabsJson`stringJSON array of `{ label, icon, content }` objects

children.text: Each tab has a label (shown in the tab bar) and content (shown in the panel):

content.code:
[
  { "label": "npm", "content": "```bash
npm install star-lite-docs
```" },
  { "label": "pnpm", "content": "```bash
pnpm add star-lite-docs
```" },
  { "label": "bun", "content": "```bash
bun add star-lite-docs
```" }
]

children.text: field with an icon name from the
Add a tabs block from the admin block picker and enter the tabs JSON. The first tab is selected by default.
Package manager commands (npm / pnpm / yarn / bun)
Star-Lite Docs ships 14 block types that cover common documentation patterns — from callout boxes and tabbed panels to file trees and code blocks.
and use the block picker to add blocks to a page. Each block type has a form with labeled fields. When
is registered in your emdash config, all 14 Star-Lite block types appear in the picker.
With Edit mode on, hover over any block to reveal the
button. Click it to open the inline property editor. You can modify all fields and save directly from the page.
to edit the full page as markdown. Standard markdown elements (headings, paragraphs, code fences, lists) are converted to the appropriate blocks. Custom block types appear as

content.html:
BlockTypeDescription
Hero`docs.hero`Splash hero with title, tagline, image, and action buttons
Image`docs.image`Standalone image with src and alt
HTML`docs.html`Raw HTML passthrough
Code`code`Syntax-highlighted code (Expressive Code)

BlockTypeDescription
[Tabs](/components/tabs)`star-lite.tabs`Tabbed content panels
[Card](/components/cards)`star-lite.card`Content card with optional icon
[Card Grid](/components/card-grids)`star-lite.cardGrid`Grid layout for cards
[Link Card](/components/link-cards)`star-lite.linkCard`Navigation card with link
[Aside](/components/asides)`star-lite.aside`Callout box (note, tip, caution, danger)
[Badge](/components/badges)`star-lite.badge`Inline status badge
[File Tree](/components/file-tree)`star-lite.fileTree`Directory tree display
[Icon](/components/icons)`star-lite.icon`Inline icon
[Link Button](/components/link-buttons)`star-lite.linkButton`Styled link as a button
[Steps](/components/steps)`star-lite.steps`Numbered step-by-step instructions

children.text: Many blocks support inline visual editing. Elements with the
when Edit mode is on. Changes are synced to the block's properties and saved when you click Save in the toolbar.
for a complete list of fields for each block type.
The CLI handles everything — D1, R2, deploy, admin user creation, and seed content:

content.code: bun create star-lite-docs   # choose Cloudflare Workers → Start now
bun create star-lite-docs   # choose Docker → Start now

docker build -t <project-name> .
docker run -d --restart unless-stopped -p 4321:4321 \
  -v <project-name>-data:/app/data \
  -v <project-name>-uploads:/app/uploads \
  <project-name>
bun create star-lite-docs   # choose Local development → Start now

children.text: . Edit docs visually in the browser — no MDX files, no Git commits, no rebuilds.

content.code: curl -fsSL https://bun.sh/install | bash
bun create star-lite-docs

children.text: The CLI scaffolds your project and offers three deployment targets:

content.content: Star-Lite also works with any existing emdash site — just run `bunx astro add star-lite-docs`. Block components, the welcome page, and route conflict warnings are all handled automatically at build time.

Star-Lite Docs offers three ways to edit content. All require logging in at
and toggling Edit mode in the toolbar.
With Edit mode on, click any text on the page to edit it in place. Text elements use
— just type and your changes appear immediately. A
button appears in the toolbar when you have unsaved changes.
Visual editing works on any element with the
attribute. Most block types support inline editing for their primary text fields (titles, taglines, content).
Hover over any block to reveal an
button in the top-right corner. Clicking it opens an inline property editor showing all the block's fields — titles, URLs, JSON configuration, and more.
Make your changes in the form and click
. The form writes back to the block's
and triggers a save to the API.
This is the best way to edit structured fields like hero actions, tab configurations, or link card URLs that aren't directly visible as text on the page.
in the page header to switch to a full-page markdown editor. The entire page content is serialized to markdown using emdash's
Custom block types (hero, cards, tabs, etc.) appear as
fences in the markdown. You can edit the JSON inside these fences or leave them alone — they round-trip cleanly on save.
to convert the markdown back to Portable Text and publish. Click
to return to the normal view without saving.
button in the page header copies the current page as markdown to your clipboard. This is useful for migrating content out of the CMS or sharing page content as plain text.
Text blocks support standard markdown formatting:
Headings are automatically converted to HTML with
attributes for table of contents anchors. The TOC on the right side of the page is built from h2 and h3 headings.
Fenced code blocks with a language identifier are rendered by Expressive Code with Night Owl syntax themes:

content.code: const greeting = "Hello, Star-Lite!";

children.text: Markdown tables are converted to HTML tables during preprocessing:

content.html:
FeatureStatus
Visual editingSupported
Block editorSupported
Markdown modeSupported

children.text: Both visual editing and block editing save through the same flow:
to remove FTS indexes (prevents corruption during writes)
The page reloads to show the updated content

content.content: emdash returns HTTP 200 with `{ success: false }` on failed writes, not a 4xx status. The save handler checks both `res.ok` and `body.success` to detect failures correctly.

children.text: Star-Lite Docs uses CSS custom properties (prefixed
) for theming. These are defined in
and can be overridden to customize the look of your site.
Dark mode is the default. Light mode values are set under
) are also available via hue variables like

content.code:
:root {
  --sl-text-2xs: 0.75rem;   /* 12px */
  --sl-text-xs: 0.8125rem;  /* 13px */
  --sl-text-sm: 0.875rem;   /* 14px */
  --sl-text-base: 1rem;     /* 16px */
  --sl-text-lg: 1.125rem;   /* 18px */
  --sl-text-xl: 1.25rem;    /* 20px */
  --sl-text-2xl: 1.5rem;    /* 24px */
  --sl-text-3xl: 1.8125rem; /* 29px */
  --sl-text-4xl: 2.1875rem; /* 35px */
  --sl-text-5xl: 2.625rem;  /* 42px */
  --sl-text-6xl: 4rem;      /* 64px */

  --sl-line-height: 1.75;
  --sl-line-height-headings: 1.2;
}

This is the fallback title. If you set a site title in the emdash admin (Settings > Site > Title), that takes precedence on every request — no rebuild needed.
By default, the sidebar is loaded from the
emdash menu at request time. To use a static sidebar instead, pass a
with Night Owl dark/light themes for syntax highlighting. You can customize it or disable it:

When a page's first block is a
, the layout automatically switches to splash mode: the sidebar and table of contents are hidden, and the content area is widened. This is used for the welcome page.
Star-Lite supports dark, light, and auto (system preference) themes via the theme selector in the page header. The theme uses CSS custom properties prefixed with
Pages in Star-Lite Docs are stored as rows in the
database table. Each page has a title, slug, content (Portable Text blocks), and a publication status.
to create a new page. Fill in:
— displayed in the browser tab and as a heading
— add blocks using the block picker
to make the page publicly visible.
The slug determines the public URL. A page with slug
Slugs can contain slashes to create logical grouping:
. These are purely URL-based — there's no directory structure in the database.
The catch-all route only renders pages where
. Unpublished drafts return a 404 on the public site.
exists, the site root shows a themed splash page with Houston (the Starlight mascot) and links to create your first page and edit the sidebar. This is a real
render with a 404 status — not a blank error page.
Looks up a published page by slug
Preprocesses blocks (headings get TOC anchors, images get normalized, tables get converted to HTML)
with the sidebar, TOC, and theme
If no page matches, it returns a 404 with a styled "Page not found" message inside the docs layout.
The page title in the browser tab follows the pattern:
The site title is resolved at request time: emdash's Settings > Site > Title takes precedence, falling back to the
. This means you can rename your site from the admin without redeploying.
A Star-Lite Docs project is a standard Astro project with emdash and star-lite-docs as integrations. Here's the typical layout:

content.code:
my-docs/
├── astro.config.mjs     # Astro config with starLiteDocs() + emdash()
├── package.json
├── tsconfig.json
├── .emdash/
│   └── seed.json        # Optional custom seed (pages, menus)
├── public/
│   └── houston.webp     # Auto-copied by the integration
├── data.db              # SQLite database (created at runtime)
├── uploads/             # Uploaded media files
├── Dockerfile           # Docker deployment (if scaffolded)
└── src/
    └── pages/
        └── .gitkeep     # Empty — routes are injected by the integration

my-docs/
├── astro.config.mjs     # starLiteDocs() + emdash() with d1/r2
├── package.json
├── tsconfig.json
├── wrangler.jsonc       # D1 database and R2 bucket bindings
├── worker-configuration.d.ts
├── .emdash/
│   └── seed.json        # Optional custom seed
├── public/
│   └── houston.webp
└── src/
    └── pages/
        └── .gitkeep

children.text: The main configuration file. This is where
Optional. Overrides the default seed content and menus. See
is auto-copied here by the integration on first build. Add your own images, favicons, etc.
Usually empty. Star-Lite injects its routes via the integration — you don't need to create page files. If you add your own route files here (e.g.
), the integration will warn about conflicts.
All content, users, menus, and settings are stored here.
Star-Lite Docs automatically creates the database schema and default content the first time your site receives a request.
On every request, Star-Lite's middleware calls
Checks a module-level flag — if the seed has already been applied, it returns immediately
Loads any user seed via emdash's virtual seed module and merges it with the default seed
menu only if it doesn't already exist
— existing collections, fields, and menus are never overwritten. Only missing pieces are added.

content.content:
The middleware runs with `order: "pre"` so it executes **before** emdash's auth middleware. Without this, emdash would redirect unauthenticated requests to `/setup` and the bootstrap would never fire on a fresh install.

On a cold database, the first request fails with "no such table" because emdash hasn't migrated yet. The second request succeeds. This is logged as `[star-lite-docs] bootstrap seed failed` then `applied default seed`.

children.text: file with starter content and sidebar links. emdash exposes this via
, and the bootstrap merges it with the default seed:
Collections and fields always come from the default seed
). Note: the runtime middleware uses an inline version in the virtual module, not this export.
Star-Lite Docs supports two sidebar modes:
(default) where the sidebar is editable from the admin UI, and
where you define it in your Astro config.
By default, the sidebar reads from the
emdash menu. This is created automatically on first boot.
to add, remove, and reorder sidebar links.
become links within their parent group

content.code:
docs-sidebar
├── Getting Started → /getting-started
├── Guides (group)
│   ├── First Guide → /guides/first
│   └── Second Guide → /guides/second
└── Reference (group)
    └── API → /reference/api

children.text: This renders a sidebar with "Getting Started" as a top-level link, then "Guides" and "Reference" as collapsible groups.

content.content: Changes to the menu take effect immediately on the next page load — no rebuild needed. This is one of the main advantages of the menu-driven approach.

children.text: to use a fixed sidebar defined at build time:
When a static sidebar is provided, the
menu is ignored entirely. The sidebar is baked into the build via a virtual module.
The current page is automatically highlighted in the sidebar. Star-Lite compares the current URL path against each sidebar link's
(with trailing slashes normalized) and marks the matching entry as active.
Star-Lite Docs includes built-in search powered by a
endpoint that queries the database directly.
on Mac) to open the search modal. Type at least 2 characters to search across all published pages by title and content.
Results show the page title, a snippet with the matched text highlighted, and link to the page.
Extracts a text snippet from Portable Text blocks with
This is a simple substring search — there's no full-text search indexing, stemming, or ranking. It works well for small to medium documentation sites.
The endpoint is public (no authentication required) and returns JSON:

content.code: GET /api/search?q=sidebar

children.text: Queries shorter than 2 characters return an empty results array.
endpoint that drops SQLite full-text search indexes and triggers on the pages table. This is called automatically before every save operation to prevent FTS index corruption when content is updated.
queries directly, so search continues to work regardless of FTS state.

content.tagline: The Starlight documentation experience, powered by EmDash CMS. Edit docs visually in the browser.

children.text: — collapsible sidebar, table of contents, Ctrl+K search, dark/light/auto theme

content.code: bunx astro add star-lite-docs

children.text: . If installing from GitHub instead of npm:

content.code: bun add github:gruntlord5/star-lite-docs

// add to your integrations array:
starLiteDocs({ title: "My Docs" }),

children.text: Block components, the welcome page, and route conflict warnings are all handled automatically at build time.

content.code:
starLiteDocs,        // Astro integration (blocks auto-registered at build)
  starLiteBlocks,      // emdash plugin descriptor (only needed for advanced setups)
  preprocessBlocks,    // PT normalizer (headings, tables, images, text → docs.html)
  preprocessImages,    // image-only subset
  loadSidebarFromMenu, // resolve emdash menu → SidebarConfig[]
  ensurePagesCollection, // idempotent seed bootstrap
  defaultSeed,         // the SeedFile shipped by the plugin
  buildSidebar,        // build sidebar tree from flat config
  markCurrent,         // mark the active sidebar item by slug
} from "star-lite-docs";

  StarLiteDocsOptions,
  StarLiteDocsConfig,
  SidebarConfig,
  SidebarEntry,
  SidebarLink,
  SidebarGroup,
} from "star-lite-docs";

children.text: Astro components are available at subpath imports:
All public exports are available from the
package entry point. Astro components use subpath imports.
The main Astro integration. Injects routes, middleware, Expressive Code, and the virtual config module.
Also available as the default export, so
that registers Star-Lite's block types in the admin UI.
Normalizes Portable Text blocks for rendering: converts headings to HTML with TOC anchors, converts
blocks, converts markdown tables to HTML, and wraps standard text blocks as
blocks. Mutates the array in place.
Loads sidebar configuration from an emdash menu. Defaults to
Top-level items with children become sidebar groups. Top-level items without children become lone links in an implicit root group.
. Merges the default seed with any user seed, applies collections/fields/content, and creates the
menu if absent. Skips after the first successful call.
Note: the runtime middleware uses an inline reimplementation in
instead of the filesystem). This export is the file-based version for programmatic use.
object shipped by the plugin. Contains the
set on the entry matching the given path.

content.code:
StarLiteDocsOptions,

  SidebarConfig,

} from "star-lite-docs";
